policy: Numerical and current claims carry an evidence class, source, access date, and caveat. Private claims are never presented as public benchmark results.

claims.claim: Primary categories 1a-1d contain 237 contribution records across 190 unique problems; 88 green full-resolution records cover 79 unique problems.
claims.caveat: Records and unique problems are different denominators.

claims.claim: The Erdős Problems AI-contribution campaign is explicitly described as not being a benchmark.
claims.caveat: Adaptive research campaigns remain scientifically useful as case collections.

claims.claim: FrontierMath v2 contains 338 problems: 295 in Tiers 1-3 and 43 in Tier 4; Epoch reports that errors were addressed in 42 percent of problems.
claims.caveat: Old and v2 model percentages are not directly comparable; the Tier-4 private evaluation set contains 41 problems because two of the 43 are public.

claims.claim: The official 22 June 2026 FrontierMath Tier-4 v2 data contains 31 task-version-2.0.0 evaluation rows. The highest reported scores are Claude Fable 5 at 87.8 +/- 5.2 percent, GPT-5.5 Pro at 78.0 +/- 6.5 percent, and AI Co-Mathematician at 75.6 +/- 6.7 percent.
claims.caveat: The evaluated systems differ in accessibility, scaffolding, tools, and inference compute; the 41-problem private set gives wide and overlapping standard errors.

claims.claim: Aletheia reports majority expert acceptance for 6 of 10 FirstProof problems; problem 8 was not unanimous.
claims.caveat: The score is best-of-two across two base models. P8 received 5 of 7 Correct ratings; each agent also produced at least one false positive.

claims.claim: Aletheia received the ten FirstProof LaTeX problem statements without modification. A predetermined verifier/extractor produced LaTeX submissions, the researchers did not ask the model for clarification, and each candidate received at least two independent academic assessments.
claims.caveat: The authors interpret Correct as publishable after minor revisions; FirstProof itself was not intended as a formal benchmark.

claims.claim: The 2026 AI Co-Mathematician is presented as a stateful asynchronous workbench with tools and human uncertainty management.
claims.caveat: Earlier v1 scores describe a superseded system and protocol.

claims.claim: Monticone gives a complete classification for Nathanson's questions, autonomously discovered and Lean-verified by Aristotle.
claims.caveat: Human authors retain review, exposition, and responsibility.

claims.claim: The Leiden Declaration on Artificial Intelligence and Mathematics was released on 2 June 2026 and is endorsed by the International Mathematical Union.
OpenAI released GPT-5.4 in ChatGPT, the API, and Codex on 5 March 2026.
claims.caveat: Provider benchmark claims are attributed to the provider.

claims.claim: OpenAI reports a model-assisted disproof of a central conjecture in the planar unit-distance problem, with a public proof and companion remarks.
claims.caveat: Use the proof record, not the announcement alone, for mathematical assessment.

claims.claim: At commit 9633e5a, ZsigmondyChebyshev/Main.lean has 4596 lines, 187 theorem or lemma declarations, no sorry or admit token, and culminates in Satz_2: existence of a primitive prime divisor is equivalent to exclusion of eight encoded exceptional families.
claims.caveat: This lecture audited the source snapshot but did not perform an independent cold Mathlib build; paper-to-Lean fidelity of the exception predicate remains a human semantic audit.

claims.claim: At commit c177541, fineqs/Main.lean has 647 lines, 10 theorem or lemma declarations, no sorry or admit token, and proves a zero-set-preserving compression theorem plus sharpness and affine/projective corollaries.
claims.caveat: The project blueprint explicitly says Aristotle formalized a slightly different version of auxiliary Lemma 5; this lecture did not perform an independent cold Mathlib build.

claims.claim: The EML project archive reports more than 25 Aristotle submissions in five waves over 36 hours; in one 15-submission wave, nine returned a clean proof on the first pass. Failed runs triggered diagnosis and theorem-domain changes before resubmission.
claims.caveat: These are project-specific process measurements, not a general benchmark of Aristotle or autoformalization systems.

claims.claim: The audited EML release records 36 of 36 target primitives, 100 public theorems, zero sorry/admit in the verified surface, and 8062 dispatched jobs.
claims.caveat: Metrics describe the audited release surface, not every historical branch.

claims.claim: The promoted ECDSA.fail record has score 1677861900, 1434070 Toffoli gates, 1170 peak qubits, and 9024 trusted shots with zero recorded failures; the score is 84.3 percent below the initial circuit.
claims.caveat: Challenge evidence is not a proof of correctness for every possible input.

claims.claim: The quartic case has profile (24,0,32,0), log-Chern numbers 112 and 40, slope 14/5 greater than 8/3, and G=208.
claims.caveat: The model label is project-reported provenance; the claim rests on exact certificates.

claims.claim: The private 17 June IGP24 snapshot contains 51 accepted distinct pairs: 5 pilot, 18 autonomous, and 28 follow-up.
claims.caveat: Ongoing competition; only aggregate counts may be published.

claims.claim: The public cryptography site exposes 45 chapters; the neural-networks site exposes 42 chapters, 329 slides, and 22 applets.
claims.caveat: Counts describe the deployed site snapshot.

claims.claim: No official OpenAI source located in this audit establishes GPT-5.6 as a released product name.
claims.caveat: The deck treats GPT-5.6 only as a project-reported label and makes no official product claim.
